result: The first light of morning is a quiet conspirator. It slips through the half-shut blinds and lands on the kitchen tiles in pale, trembling stripes, like a child drawing with water. I stand barefoot, kettle rattling, and let the day arrange itself around me: one blue mug, two spoons of coffee grounds, the neighbor’s dog coughing once, sharply, as if clearing its throat for the starring role in a radio play. Time hasn’t yet hardened into schedule; minutes are still soft wax I can thumb into any shape. I choose to stretch them, to pocket a few for later when the inbox begins its hungry chirping. Outside, the maple is releasing its helicopters, dozens spinning down like green coins flipped by an unseen hand. I catch one, split the wing, and stick it on my fingertip—an impromptu puppet, a single-winged biplane. For a second I am seven again, convinced that if I launch it from the balcony it will circle the planet and return with postal stamps from Jakarta and a polar bear’s signature. The kettle clicks off; the spell thins but doesn’t break. I pour, stir, breathe steam. Somewhere higher than the roofline, a jet draws its white line across the sky’s blue notebook, and I imagine the passengers up there drinking thin coffee from plastic cups, rehearsing arrival speeches, unaware that a woman in pajamas is wishing them wind. I raise my mug in salute, sip bitterness, feel the future settle on my shoulders like a cat that refuses to be ignored.

I stand barefoot, kettle rattling, and let the day arrange itself around me: one blue mug, two spoons of coffee grounds, the neighbor’s dog coughing once, sharply, as if clearing its throat for the starring role in a radio play. Time hasn’t yet hardened into schedule; minutes are still soft wax I can thumb into any shape. I choose to stretch them, to pocket a few for later when the inbox begins its hungry chirping. Outside, the maple is releasing its helicopters, dozens spinning down like green coins flipped by an unseen hand. I catch one, split the wing, and stick it on my fingertip—an impromptu puppet, a single-winged biplane. For a second I am seven again, convinced that if I launch it from the balcony it will circle the planet and return with postal stamps from Jakarta and a polar bear’s signature. The kettle clicks off; the spell thins but doesn’t break. I pour, stir, breathe steam. Somewhere higher than the roofline, a jet draws its white line across the sky’s blue notebook, and I imagine the passengers up there drinking thin coffee from plastic cups, rehearsing arrival speeches, unaware that a woman in pajamas is wishing them wind. I raise my mug in salute, sip bitterness, feel the future settle on my shoulders like a cat that refuses to be ignored.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
